text.tspan: 05. 1650. Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
A: Since Node has left, right, and parent pointers,
and p and q are instances or Node. We can find the
depths of p and q by traversing up from them to
the root of the tree while tracking their depth.
Once the depths of p and q are found we define
p' and q' as copies p and q respectively. Then
we move the pointers p' and q' up their branch
until they are at the same depth. For example
if p' is 5 levels deeper than q' we move p' five
Once p' and q' are at the same level. We move them
up simultaneously until they point to the same
node. This node is then returned, as it is the
If p' and q' end up pointing to None it means
that they past the root of their respective
branches, and no common ancestor was found.

- If heights[i] > maxH append i to oceanViews

A: Notice that this problem can be generalized as:
Given a string s, return True if s can become
a palindrome after deleting at most k characters.
Thus, this can be solved by implementing a function
isKPalindrome(s, k) that returns True if s can
become a palindrome if we change at most k chars,
and we call it with k=1.
This is a classic recursion problem.
if i == None and j == None:
return isKPalindrome(s, k - 1, i + 1, j) or\
08. 1570. Dot Product of Two Sparse Vectors
A: A vector is sparse if most of its elements are
zero. Thus, our SparseVector class should only
encode non-zero elements to save space. To do so,
we can define a private variable self.inner = {}
to map the indexes of the vector to their respec-
tive values, as long as the values are non-zero.
This representation allows for a nice property:
when dot multiplying two vectors U and V, we only
need to iterate over the indexes in U.inner as
they are guarenteed to map to non-zero values, if
V.inner[i] exists then it is compounded into the
if i not in V.inner: continue
